title: Coronation chicken
subtitle: Cold chicken in curried mayonnaise with apricot.
excerpt: Poached chicken, off the bone, folded into a curried mayonnaise sharpened with apricot purée, lemon, and a splash of red wine. The cold lunch invented for the 1953 coronation banquet, now a sandwich filling and a picnic staple.
sourceNotes: Constance Spry and Rosemary Hume created the dish for the 1953 coronation luncheon at Westminster Hall. The recipe became a standard of British picnic and buffet cookery through the 1960s and 70s.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Better the next day. Make 12 to 24 hours ahead and keep refrigerated.

content.text: A whole chicken poached gently in a pan of water with carrot, celery, onion, bay, and peppercorn until just tender. The meat is stripped from the bone and cooled, then folded into a sauce of mayonnaise, soured cream, curry paste, apricot jam, lemon juice, and a teaspoon of red wine. Slivered toasted almonds at the end, a scattering of coriander. Cold, on a bed of rice, on a sandwich, in a pita.

The Constance Spry original used a fresh curry paste built from softening onion, garlic, tomato purée, and curry powder; modern versions can shortcut that with a tablespoon of mild curry paste from a jar. The flavour of the original is worth the extra ten minutes.
What you need

items.prepNote: small, finely chopped, for the sauce

content.text: A stockpot for the chicken. A small frying pan for the curry-paste base. A large bowl for the mixing. The blender helps smooth the apricot, but mashing with a fork works fine.
Method
Poach the chicken

Sit the chicken in the stockpot. Add the chunked carrot, celery, halved onion, bay leaves, and peppercorns. Cover with cold water — about 2.5 litres. Bring to a gentle simmer; skim any foam. Cook 50 minutes on the lowest heat. Lift out, let cool 15 minutes, then strip all the meat off the bones in good-sized pieces. Aim for around 700 g of meat. Keep in the fridge while you make the sauce. The poaching liquid keeps as a stock for another day.
Build the curry base

Heat {{vegetable-oil}} of oil in a small frying pan over medium heat. Add the chopped small {{onion}} of onion. Cook 8 minutes until soft. Stir in {{curry-powder}} of curry powder and {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée for 1 minute. Pour in {{red-wine}} of red wine and the juice of {{lemon}} of lemon. Add {{dried-apricots}} of chopped dried apricots and {{honey}} of honey. Simmer 4 minutes until the apricots soften. Tip into a heatproof bowl and cool to room temperature.
Make the sauce

Blend the cooled apricot mixture smooth with a stick blender, or push through a sieve with the back of a spoon. Tip into a large bowl. Whisk in {{mayonnaise}} of mayonnaise, {{soured-cream}} of soured cream, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Taste; the sauce should sit between curried, fruity, sharp, and creamy.
Fold in the chicken
Add the cooled chicken to the bowl. Fold gently with a spatula until every piece is coated. Cover and refrigerate at least 1 hour, or overnight.
Toast the almonds
Tip {{almonds-flaked}} of flaked almonds into a dry frying pan over medium heat. Toast for 3 minutes, shaking the pan, until pale gold. Tip onto a plate to stop the cooking.
Serve

Tip onto a serving platter or into a wide bowl. Scatter with the toasted almonds and {{coriander}} of chopped coriander. Serve cold or at cool room temperature with rice, in a sandwich on granary bread, or in a wrap for a picnic.
Troubleshooting

items.cause: Poached too hard, or for too long.
items.fix: The water should barely tremble on the surface. Pull the bird out at 50 minutes; let it cool in the broth for the first 15 if anything.
Add the juice of the second half of lemon and another half teaspoon of salt. The balance is curried-tart-fruity, not sugary.

items.cause: Curry base not cooled before mixing.
items.fix: Always cool the apricot-curry base fully before whisking into the mayonnaise. Warmth thins the sauce and risks splitting.
Strip the chicken into chunks the size of a walnut, with a mix of breast and thigh meat. Texture matters.

content.text: Where this dish lives

Coronation chicken was created by Constance Spry and Rosemary Hume for the 1953 coronation banquet at Westminster Hall, to feed 350 foreign dignitaries a dish that could be prepared ahead and served cold. The original ("Poulet Reine Elizabeth") used fresh curry paste and a fruit purée; the modern household version simplifies both. It belongs to the British summer picnic basket alongside scotch eggs, pork pies, and a pot of homemade chutney.
